module.title: Module 7: Creation of Metes and Bounds Descriptions
module.description: How a legally sufficient metes-and-bounds land description is built and read — caption, point of beginning, the body of courses (bearings and distances), calls for monuments, closure, area, and certification — with bearings/azimuths, latitude/departure, and closure math, made Texas-specific (GLO original-survey/abstract ties, vara-based historic field notes, and the TBPELS standards in 22 TAC Ch. 138).
learning_objectives: Define the key terms and principles used in metes-and-bounds land descriptions, and distinguish metes (measures) from bounds (adjoiners).
Construct an accurate legal land description using a caption, point of beginning, bearings, distances, monument calls, a closing call, and an area/certification.
Evaluate a metes-and-bounds description for accuracy, legal sufficiency (must close, be locatable, and be unambiguous), and compliance with statutory and TBPELS requirements.
Perform the supporting computations — bearing↔azimuth and back-azimuth, latitude and departure of a course, linear misclosure, and parcel area.
module.textbook_chapters: Brown ch. 5 (Creation of Metes and Bounds Descriptions); see also ch. 4 (Creation of Boundaries)

lessons.title: Metes, Bounds, and the Anatomy of a Description
learning_objectives: Distinguish metes from bounds and name the principal types of land description.
Identify the parts of a written description: caption, body, closing, area, and certification.
State the foundational principles that govern how a description creates a boundary.

content.html:
What a description has to do

A metes-and-bounds description is a written instrument that fixes the limits of one unique parcel of land by reciting a continuous chain of measured lines around its perimeter, returning to where it began. The deed that contains it transfers an interest in real property, so the description carries a heavy burden: it must make the parties' intentions clear, must positively identify and locate one unique parcel, and must specify that parcel's size, shape, and position. If the lands are not sufficiently described, the conveyance can be void for lack of locatability. As Dr. Elgin puts it, practically all of our boundary problems can be traced back to poorly written descriptions — so the surveyor who crafts one must understand surveying math, the legal principles of boundary location, and the legal meaning of words and phrases.

Metes vs. bounds

The two halves of the name describe two different ways of fixing a line:

  • Metes are measures — the bearings (directions) and distances of consecutive lines: “thence N 42°10' E, 247.27 feet.” A pure metes description fixes the parcel entirely by recited courses and distances. This pure form has little practical application today.
  • Bounds are adjoiners — recital calls to record or physical lines, objects, or neighboring parcels that confine the parcel: “bounded on the south by State Highway No. 1; east by Freeman Addition.” A pure bounds description relies entirely on what surrounds the tract.

Most modern descriptions are combination (metes-and-bounds) descriptions: they recite bearings and distances together with bounding references to adjoiners, right-of-way lines, and monuments, returning to the point of beginning. These are the easiest to write but the hardest to retrace, because the two kinds of call can conflict — which is exactly when the priority (dignity) of calls must be applied.

rows: Recited bearings and distances of consecutive lines
Recital calls to adjoiners / physical objects
Rare today by a professional surveyor
Courses + bounding references + monuments, back to POB
Most common after an original survey or resurvey
Reference to a GLO sectional plat (“NW1/4 of the SE1/4”)
Reference to a recorded subdivision plat
A width centered on a described centerline

content.html:
The parts of a description

A complete written description has a predictable skeleton, and writing or reading it in order keeps it unambiguous:

  • Caption (preamble). The opening statement of general location — county and state, city, and the larger tract within which the parcel lies. It locates the reader and limits the extent of what follows.
  • Body. The working core: a tie to a point of commencement, the point of beginning (POB), then the sequence of courses (calls for monuments, bearings and distances, and bounds), and a closing call back “to the point of beginning.”
  • Area / quantity. A statement of acreage, almost always followed by “more or less.”
  • Limiting and augmenting clauses. “EXCEPTING THEREFROM,” “RESERVING THEREFROM,” “SUBJECT TO,” and “TOGETHER WITH” clauses that withhold, reserve, recognize, or add rights.
  • Basis of bearing and certification. The stated bearing datum and the surveyor's seal/signature.

content.text: A boundary line can be created by words in a legal document alone. That written line is as binding and as legal as if it had been run on the ground by a surveyor. This is why a description must be self-sufficient: words can create a boundary, so the words must be unambiguous.

content.html:
Foundational principles

A few settled principles govern how descriptions create and control boundaries:

  • Freedom to create, limited by others' rights. A landowner may create any boundary desired as long as it does not infringe on an adjoiner or a senior right.
  • The original survey controls. The lines and corners of the original survey control the location of a parcel; lines are presumed run in accordance with the rules and minimum standards in existence when they were created.
  • Words speak as of their own date. Words, their meanings, and recited distances are interpreted as of the date of the description, not the date of a later retracement (so “varas” in an 1850 field note still means varas).
  • Monuments must be called for. For a corner and its monument to control, the corner must be called for in the conveyance and the monument identified at the time the conveyance was made.
  • Tax deeds are not source documents. Tax statements and tax deeds are incomplete and must not be relied on as the primary source of a property description.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: A description must make intent clear, identify and locate one unique parcel, fix its size/shape/position, and not create gaps or overlaps — or the conveyance may be void.
Metes are measures (bearings and distances); bounds are adjoiners. Most modern descriptions combine the two.
Every description has a caption, a body (POC → POB → courses → closing call), an area statement, optional clauses, a basis of bearing, and certification.
Words alone can create a binding boundary; the lines and corners of the original survey control its location, interpreted as of the date written.

flashcards.definition: A written description that fixes a unique parcel by reciting a continuous series of bearings, distances, monuments, and bounding calls around the perimeter, returning to the point of beginning.
The measures of a description — the bearings (directions) and distances of the consecutive lines that make up the perimeter.
Recital calls to record or physical lines, objects, or adjoining parcels that confine (bound) the limits of the tract being described.
The opening statement of a description reciting the general location (county/state, city, larger tract) and limiting the extent of what follows in the body.

A description that recites bearings and distances together with bounding references to adjoiners, right-of-way, and monuments — the most common modern form, and the one where priority of calls matters most.

images_needed.caption: A labeled diagram of a written description broken into its parts: caption, point of commencement, point of beginning, the body of numbered courses around a simple parcel, closing call to the POB, area statement, and certification block.
Side-by-side plan views of the same parcel: one annotated only with bearings and distances (metes), one annotated only with adjoiner/road/creek bounding calls (bounds).

lessons.title: The Caption, the Point of Beginning, and Texas Ties to the GLO Survey
learning_objectives: Write a caption that locates and limits a parcel, using Texas GLO survey/abstract conventions.
Distinguish a point of commencement (POC) from a point of beginning (POB) and tie a parcel to known control.
State the Texas requirement to anchor a description to an original land-grant survey and abstract number.

content.html:
The caption: getting located

The caption (or preamble) is the first element of a description and appears first on the page. It recites the general location of the parcel and limits the extent of what the body may convey. In PLSS states a caption reads like “That part of the Northwest Quarter of the Northwest Quarter of Section 12, Township ___, Range ___, [County and State], described as follows:”. The caption also serves the title industry as an index entry. A well-formed caption is generally descriptive of the area but includes enough information that there is no possibility of mis-locating the land.

The caption and body interact. In general the body controls if it conflicts with the caption (the body is the “more particularly described” part, and the particular controls the general). But a caption can be limiting: if it says “All that part of the NW1/4 of the NW1/4 … described as follows,” a body that wanders beyond that quarter-quarter may not convey the extra land, because the caption sounds limiting.

content.text: Texas differs (PLSS): Texas has no township–range PLSS grid. Texas land was never federal public domain; titles trace to a sovereign grant (Spain, Mexico, the Republic, or the State) administered by the General Land Office. So a Texas caption is keyed to the original land-grant survey and abstract number — e.g., “situated in the John Doe Survey, Abstract No. 123, [County] County, Texas” — not to Section/Township/Range. The abstract number is the spine of Texas land identification.

content.html:
Commencing versus beginning

Descriptions distinguish two starting points:

  • A point of commencement (POC) is a well-established, easily found point on the ground — often a section/survey corner or other monument of record — that may not be a corner of the parcel. You commence there because it is reliably locatable, then run a tie line to the parcel.
  • The point of beginning (POB) (sometimes “true point of beginning”) is an actual corner of the parcel being described. A description should have one and only one POB. If commencement is itself a corner of the parcel, you can note it is “being also the point of beginning.” If the description does not close back on the POB, the ending point is differentiated as a point of terminus (POT).

Modern guidance favors “Commencing at …” on essentially all descriptions, proceeding to the “Point of Beginning,” and reserving “BEGINNING” for the one true start. This avoids any confusion over which point is the true point of beginning.

sections.title: Worked caption + commencement (PLSS-style example from the slides)
sections.content:
“That part of the Northwest Quarter of the Northwest Quarter of Section 12, Township (number), Range (number), (County & State) described as follows: Commencing at the northwest corner of said Northwest Quarter of the Northwest Quarter; thence on an assumed bearing of South 00 degrees 10 minutes 00 seconds West along the west line of said Northwest Quarter of the Northwest Quarter 330.00 feet to the point of beginning; thence South 89 degrees 50 minutes 00 seconds East 660.00 feet; … to the point of beginning.”

Notice the structure: caption (general location) → commencement at a found corner → a tie course (330.00 ft) to reach the POB → the body of courses → closing call. The “assumed bearing” phrase states the basis of bearing. In Texas, the caption would instead read “situated in the [grantee] Survey, Abstract No. ___, [County] County, Texas,” and the commencement would tie to a corner of that original GLO survey.

content.html:
Tie to control — the Texas way

A description is only as locatable as its tie to known control. By Texas convention the POB and at least one controlling corner are tied back to a corner of the original GLO survey/abstract, so the parcel can be re-located independently of the surveyor's own work. The governing rule, 22 TAC §138.91, requires that courses be “referenced … to an identifiable and monumented line or an established geodetic system.” Today that basis of bearing is usually grid — the Texas State Plane Coordinate System on the NAD83 datum, by zone — while many historic descriptions used magnetic or assumed bearings (a known source of rotation discrepancies in retracement). The statutory hook is Occupations Code §1071.002, which defines “state land surveying” to include “determining by survey the location or relocation of original land grant boundaries and corners.”

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: The caption locates and limits the parcel; the body controls a conflicting caption, but a limiting caption can cap what the body conveys.
A POC is a reliably found point that may not be a parcel corner; the POB is the one true corner where the perimeter starts. Use “Commencing at” then “Point of Beginning.”
Texas captions key to the original GLO survey + abstract number, not Section/Township/Range — Texas has no PLSS grid.
Tie the POB and a controlling corner to the original survey and state the basis of bearing (today usually Texas State Plane / NAD83), per 22 TAC §138.91.

flashcards.definition: A well-established, easily found point on the ground used to start a description; it may not be an actual corner of the parcel. A tie course leads from the POC to the point of beginning.
An actual corner of the parcel being described, where the perimeter run starts and to which it closes. A description should have exactly one POB.
The differentiated ending point used when a description (such as a strip or line description) does not close back on the same point at which it began.

The identifying number the Texas GLO assigns to an original land-grant survey; it is the spine of Texas land identification and is recited in the caption of a Texas description (e.g., “Abstract No. 123”).

The stated reference to which all bearings in a description are oriented — an identifiable monumented line or an established geodetic system (today, commonly the Texas State Plane Coordinate System, NAD83); must be stated per 22 TAC §138.91(c).

images_needed.caption: Plan view showing a found survey/section corner as POC, a dashed tie course to the parcel, the POB at the first true corner, and the parcel perimeter running from the POB.
Schematic of a parcel located inside an original GLO survey labeled with grantee name and Abstract No., contrasted with a PLSS Section/Township/Range grid crossed out.

lessons.title: Bearings, Azimuths, and the Body of Courses
learning_objectives: Read and write a quadrant bearing and convert between a bearing and an azimuth (and the back course).
Compute the latitude and departure of a course from its bearing and distance.
Order and phrase the calls in the body so they run cleanly around the parcel.

content.html:
Two ways to state a direction

A course is the direction of a line. Surveyors state direction two ways:

  • A quadrant bearing is measured from the North–South meridian, never exceeding 90°, and named for its quadrant: N __° __' __” E (or SE, SW, NW). Example: N 42°10' E means 42°10' east of due north.
  • An azimuth is measured clockwise from north through a full circle, 0° to 360°. North = 0° (or 360°), East = 90°, South = 180°, West = 270°.

Deeds are written in bearings; computations are often easier in azimuths. You must move fluently between them.

headers: Bearing angle β (from N or S)

content.label: Back-azimuth (reverse course): add 180° if the forward azimuth is < 180°, subtract 180° if it is ≥ 180°

content.html:
Worked conversion

Take the course S 30° 00' E (SE quadrant). Its azimuth is 180° − 30° = 150°. Walking the line the other way (the back course), the azimuth is 150° + 180° = 330°, which is N 30° 00' W — the same line, opposite direction. A back bearing always flips both letters: the reverse of S 30° E is N 30° W. This matters because a deed line and the adjoiner's deed line are the same line described from opposite ends; they should be exact reverses.

Latitude and departure

Every course can be resolved into two rectangular components: its latitude (north–south component, +N / −S) and its departure (east–west component, +E / −W). Using the bearing angle β (the number in the quadrant bearing) and the distance D:

  • Latitude = D × cos β (sign: + for a North bearing, − for a South bearing)
  • Departure = D × sin β (sign: + for an East bearing, − for a West bearing)

Latitudes and departures are the bridge from a written course to coordinates: add a course's latitude to a point's northing and its departure to the easting to get the next point. They are also the basis of the closure check in the next lesson.

content.label: Latitude and departure of a course (β = quadrant bearing angle, D = distance)

content.html:
Sequencing the body

The body recites the calls in order, running around the perimeter in either a clockwise or a counter-clockwise direction, with each course beginning “thence” (“then from there”). A few craft rules from the standards:

  • State the basis of bearing.
  • Run consistently one direction around the figure; do not jump around.
  • Bearings and distances are sometimes called finger pointings — they tell the retracing surveyor where to look for the actual corner/monument that controls.
  • End every course with a semicolon; the caption ends with a colon; the whole description ends with a period.
  • Spell bearings out and capitalize them; do not capitalize a general directional word like “northerly.” Follow the area with “more or less.”

content.text: “More or less” after a distance or area is deliberate: it signals that the figure is a supporting (informative) call, not a controlling bound, so that a found monument can still control over the recited number. Surveyors often treat bearings and distances as guides to the controlling monuments rather than as the boundary itself.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: A quadrant bearing is N/S __° E/W (≤ 90°); an azimuth is clockwise from north 0–360°. Convert by quadrant; the back course is azimuth ± 180° and flips both bearing letters.
Latitude = D·cosβ (± N/S) and Departure = D·sinβ (± E/W) resolve a course into N–S and E–W components and let you carry coordinates around the parcel.
The body runs the calls in order, clockwise or counter-clockwise, each starting “thence,” with a stated basis of bearing.
Bearings/distances are “finger pointings” to the controlling monuments; “more or less” keeps recited numbers from outranking a found monument.

flashcards.definition: A direction measured from the north or south meridian, not exceeding 90°, named for its quadrant: N __° __' __” E, S __ E, S __ W, or N __ W.
A direction measured clockwise from north through a full 360° circle (N=0°, E=90°, S=180°, W=270°).
The direction of a line viewed from the opposite end: azimuth ± 180°. The back bearing reverses both letters (the reverse of S 30° E is N 30° W).
The north–south component of a course, equal to distance × cos(bearing angle); positive for North bearings, negative for South.
The east–west component of a course, equal to distance × sin(bearing angle); positive for East bearings, negative for West.
“Then from there” — the word that introduces each successive course in the body of a description.

images_needed.caption: Bearing quadrant diagram: a compass rose with N–S–E–W axes, the four quadrants labeled NE/SE/SW/NW, one example bearing N 42°10' E drawn, and the equivalent azimuth arc shown clockwise from north.
A single course drawn as the hypotenuse of a right triangle, with the latitude (vertical, D·cosβ) and departure (horizontal, D·sinβ) legs labeled and the bearing angle β marked from north.

lessons.title: Closure, Sufficiency, and Common Defects
learning_objectives: Compute the linear misclosure of a traverse from the sums of latitudes and departures.
State the three sufficiency requirements: a description must close, be locatable, and be unambiguous.
Recognize common defects and apply the dignity of calls to evaluate a description.

content.html:
Closure: the figure must come back

A metes-and-bounds description traces a closed polygon. If you start at the POB, sum every course's latitude and every course's departure, and walk all the way around, you should end exactly back at the POB. In a perfect figure the sum of all latitudes is zero and the sum of all departures is zero. In a real (or recited) traverse they rarely close perfectly; the small leftover is the misclosure.

  • Closure in latitude = ΣLat (should be 0)
  • Closure in departure = ΣDep (should be 0)
  • Linear misclosure = √(ΣLat² + ΣDep²) — the straight-line gap between the computed end point and the POB

A description that does not mathematically close, with no gap or overlap, is legally defective. (The deed in Karr v. Ray literally would not close until the parties agreed two bearings had been written W instead of E.)

The three sufficiency requirements

To be legally sufficient a description must satisfy three tests, all reinforced by 22 TAC §138.91, which requires “a definite and unambiguous identification of the location of such boundaries”:

  • It must close. The calls must return to the POB forming a closed figure with no gap or overlap. For a Texas Category 1A survey a separate closure report is submitted, and traverse/closure tolerances are set by 22 TAC §138.83 (Precision and Accuracy) and the TSPS Manual of Practice.
  • It must be locatable. A competent surveyor must be able to go to the ground and re-establish the lines from the writing — which requires a tie to control, monument calls, and a basis of bearing.
  • It must be unambiguous. It must describe one and only one parcel, with intent clear on its face.

Ambiguity, and how it is cured

Classic doctrine distinguished a latent ambiguity (not visible on the face of the description but appearing when it is applied to the ground — curable with extrinsic evidence) from a patent ambiguity (an ambiguity so severe the parties' intent cannot be determined). The modern tendency is to uphold the validity of a deed if it is at all possible to arrive at the parties' intent, and to allow almost any extrinsic evidence to help locate the lands described — not to change what is written, but to aid in locating it.

rows: Leaves a gap/overlap; figure is mathematically and legally defective
No tie to control / no basis of bearing
Not locatable; cannot be re-established on the ground
A corner placed on a curved line
A boundary (property) corner should not be set on a curved line
Tax deeds are incomplete and are not a proper source description
Conflicting calls (monument vs. bearing vs. distance)
Requires applying the dignity of calls to resolve
Latent ambiguity; needs extrinsic evidence or may fail

content.html:
Dignity of calls — the tie-breaker

When calls in one description conflict, they are ranked by reliability (“dignity”), highest to lowest: natural monuments → artificial monuments → adjoinder → course (bearing) → distance → quantity/area. This is a rule of construction that always yields to the parties' intent and to the footsteps of the original surveyor. It is why a found, called-for, undisturbed monument controls over a conflicting distance, and why a stated acreage is the weakest call of all — a check, not a control.

sections.title: Texas case: State v. Franks (113 S.W.2d 589, Tex. Civ. App. 1937) — footsteps & senior rights
sections.content: The Texas court fixed the north line of the senior Ransom House Survey by crediting surveyor Atkinson, who “followed the footsteps of the original surveyor as carefully, closely, and accurately as might be expected,” while rejecting later surveys that did not follow those footsteps. The court restated the settled rule that “a junior survey must be made to conform to and harmonize with the lines and calls for older surveys, because the boundaries of the older surveys cannot be extended or varied to satisfy the calls of a junior survey.” The lesson for description-writing: a new junior description must respect the senior boundary and the original surveyor's footsteps — it cannot enlarge itself by its own recited courses.

content.text: Texas standards note: The old 22 TAC Chapter 663 surveying standards were REPEALED. After HB 1523 (86th Leg., 2019) merged the surveying board into TBPELS, the standards were re-codified (eff. 2021) into 22 TAC Chapter 138, “Compliance and Professionalism for Surveyors.” The Professional and Technical Standards are in 22 TAC Ch. 138, Subchapter E (§§138.81 et seq.). Any material citing §663 is outdated for 2026.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Sum the latitudes and departures; the linear misclosure = √(ΣLat² + ΣDep²). A description that will not close (no gap/overlap) is defective.
Sufficiency = close + locatable + unambiguous, all required by 22 TAC §138.91; closure tolerances live in §138.83 and the TSPS Manual.
The modern tendency favors upholding a deed and using extrinsic evidence to locate (not rewrite) ambiguous lands.
When calls conflict, apply the dignity of calls (natural → artificial → adjoinder → course → distance → area), subordinate to intent and the original surveyor's footsteps.

flashcards.definition: The requirement that a description's courses return exactly to the point of beginning, forming a closed figure; in a perfect traverse ΣLat = 0 and ΣDep = 0.
The straight-line gap between the computed end point of a traverse and the point of beginning, equal to √(ΣLat² + ΣDep²).
The three legal requirements that a description must close, must be locatable on the ground, and must unambiguously describe one unique parcel.
Latent ambiguity does not appear on the face of a description but emerges when it is applied (curable with extrinsic evidence); patent ambiguity is so severe the parties' intent cannot be determined.

The reliability ranking used to resolve conflicting calls: natural monuments > artificial monuments > adjoinder > course (bearing) > distance > quantity/area — all subordinate to intent and the original surveyor's footsteps.

The highest-standard boundary/land-title survey in Texas (defined by the TSPS Manual of Practice and incorporated by the TBPELS rules); requires a certification of substantial compliance and a separate closure report.

images_needed.caption: A four-course traverse drawn from the POB; the courses fail to return exactly to the POB, with the small gap dimensioned as the linear misclosure and ΣLat / ΣDep arrows shown.

A vertical priority-of-calls ladder: natural monument at top, then artificial monument, adjoinder, course, distance, and quantity/area at the bottom, with intent and footsteps shown as overriding both sides.

lessons.title: Texas Conventions: Varas, the GLO, and Putting a Description Together
learning_objectives: Convert vara-based historic field notes to feet and explain why the original units govern.
Describe the role of the GLO and the credentials and standards that govern Texas descriptions.
Assemble a complete, compliant Texas metes-and-bounds description and add the proper clauses and certification.

content.html:
Vara-based historic descriptions

Many original Texas field notes are written in varas (abbreviated “vrs.”). Texas law fixed the vara at exactly 33⅓ inches by Art. 5730, Revised Civil Statutes (Acts of 1919, eff. June 17, 1919). Because words and distances speak as of the date written, a retracing surveyor must convert the historic varas to feet — and must reconcile the often magnetic or assumed historic bearing basis to a modern datum.

  • 1 vara = 33⅓ in = 2.7778 ft (equivalently, varas × 2.7778 = feet; feet × 0.36 = varas)
  • Example: “S 45° E, 950 vrs” = 950 × 2.7778 ≈ 2,638.9 ft

Larger historic units: 1 labor = 1,000,000 sq varas ≈ 177.1 acres; 1 league (sitio) = 25 labors ≈ 4,428 acres.

content.text: Texas differs: The vara is essentially unique to Texas and former Spanish-grant areas. Its historical value varied (Spanish “Vara de Burgos” ~32.9 in; Mexican vara ~32.99 in), but Texas statutorily uses 33⅓ in. Do not blindly apply a foreign vara value to a Texas grant — the resulting acreage will be wrong.

content.html:
The General Land Office

The General Land Office (GLO) is the custodian of Texas's original land-grant records — patents, field notes, sketches, and maps — dating to the Spanish/Mexican period. A surveyor retracing an original survey must consult the GLO record to locate the original grant boundaries and corners, then follow the original surveyor's footsteps on the ground. A patent is the instrument that severs land from the sovereign. Original surveys of state land, and corrections to original grant boundaries, require a Licensed State Land Surveyor (LSLS), a higher GLO-issued credential than RPLS.

Who may write a description, and under what standards

The governing statute is Occupations Code Chapter 1071 (the Professional Land Surveying Practices Act). Only a Registered Professional Land Surveyor (RPLS) (or an LSLS) may prepare a metes-and-bounds description, delineate and monument boundaries, and seal and sign the survey. The administrative standards are in 22 TAC Part 6, Chapter 138, with the Professional and Technical Standards in Subchapter E:

  • §138.83 — Precision and Accuracy (traverse/positional tolerances; closure)
  • §138.87 — Monumentation (set monuments of a stable, permanent nature; document found vs. set, marked traceably to the registrant)
  • §138.91 — Survey Drawing / Written Description / Report (unambiguous identification; state which monuments were found and which placed; reference courses to a monumented line or geodetic system)

Survey categories (such as the highest-standard Category 1A land-title survey) are defined by the TSPS Manual of Practice, which the TBPELS rules incorporate by reference.

Closing clauses, area, and certification

After the body closes back to the POB, finish the description with the proper appendages:

  • Area: “containing 22.99 acres, more or less.” Texas treats stated area as the least reliable element — a check, not a control.
  • EXCEPTING THEREFROM withholds a part not intended to be conveyed (an important device for preserving a senior right — an exception is always part of the thing granted).
  • RESERVING THEREFROM keeps a right or easement for the grantor or a stated third party (a reservation is a newly created thing).
  • SUBJECT TO recognizes an existing right (a mineral right, recorded easement, etc.); TOGETHER WITH adds an appurtenant right.
  • Call for the survey: modern practice ends with “… per plat of Survey No. ____ dated __/__/____ by ____ Surveying,” plus the basis of bearing and the surveyor's seal and signature.

sections.title: Model description (adapted from a surveyed tract)
sections.content:
“A fractional part of the [grantee] Survey, Abstract No. ___, [County] County, Texas, described as follows: Commencing at [a found corner of said survey]; thence [tie course] to a point on the [right-of-way / adjoiner line], the point of beginning of the herein described tract; thence N 87°17' W, 1002.20 feet along [said north line]; thence S 1°10' E, 611.11 feet along [said corporate limits]; thence S 73°51'30” E, 667.58 feet to the aforesaid [right-of-way]; thence northerly, 972.40 feet along the arc of a curve, concave westerly, with a radius of 975.00 feet, the chord of which is N 1°45' W, 932.60 feet, all along said right-of-way to the point of beginning. Above described tract contains 22.99 acres, more or less, per plat of Survey Number ____ dated [date], by [firm]. Basis of bearing: Texas State Plane, NAD83. END OF DESCRIPTION.”

Note the structure: Texas caption (survey + abstract) → commencement at a found corner → POB at an adjoiner → ordered courses (including a curve recited by arc, radius, and chord) bounding on a road right-of-way → closing call → area “more or less” → call for the survey → basis of bearing → certification.

links.title: 22 TAC Part 6, Ch. 138 — Compliance and Professionalism for Surveyors
links.description: TBPELS surveying standards (Subch. E: Professional and Technical Standards, §§138.81 et seq.) — current 2026 chapter; replaces repealed Ch. 663

links.title: Texas General Land Office — Land Grant Archives
links.description: Custodian of original Spanish/Mexican/Republic/State grants, patents, and field notes

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Texas field notes in varas convert at 1 vara = 2.7778 ft (feet × 0.36 = varas); use the statutory 33⅓-in vara, never a foreign value, and reconcile the historic bearing basis.
The GLO holds the original grant records a retracing surveyor must consult; state-land originals require an LSLS, and patents sever land from the sovereign.
Only an RPLS/LSLS may prepare and seal a Texas description, under 22 TAC Ch. 138 Subch. E (§§138.83, .87, .91) — not the repealed §663.
Finish with the area “more or less,” the right EXCEPTING/RESERVING/SUBJECT TO/TOGETHER WITH clauses, a call for the survey, the basis of bearing, and certification.

flashcards.definition: The historic Texas unit of length, fixed by statute at exactly 33⅓ inches (2.7778 ft); abbreviated “vrs.” in field notes. 1 labor = 1,000,000 sq varas ≈ 177.1 ac; 1 league ≈ 4,428 ac.
The instrument that severs land from the sovereign (the State of Texas or, historically, the Republic), completing an original land grant; issued and archived by the GLO.
A higher GLO-issued Texas credential (above RPLS) required to survey land in which the State or Permanent School Fund has an interest and to relocate original land-grant boundaries and corners.
A clause that withholds from the conveyance a part of the thing granted (always part of the described tract); an important device for preserving a senior right.
A clause by which the grantor keeps a newly created right or easement (for the grantor or a stated third party) out of the land conveyed.

The current (2021–) Texas Administrative Code chapter “Compliance and Professionalism for Surveyors”; its Subchapter E (§§138.81 et seq.) holds the surveying standards — it replaced the repealed Ch. 663.

images_needed.caption: A historic field-note course “S 45° E, 950 vrs” shown converting to 2,638.9 ft, with the 1 vara = 33⅓ in = 2.7778 ft scale bar.

A finished plat-and-description pairing: a labeled traverse with POC, POB, numbered courses, a curve, and bounding road right-of-way, beside the matching written Texas description with caption, area, basis of bearing, and seal block.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a land description, what is the difference between “metes” and “bounds”?
options: Metes are the measures (bearings and distances); bounds are the adjoiners (calls to neighboring lines/objects)
Metes are natural monuments; bounds are artificial monuments
Metes are the area; bounds are the perimeter
Metes are used in Texas; bounds are used only in PLSS states
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Metes are the measures (bearings and distances); bounds are the adjoiners (calls to neighboring lines/objects)
quiz_questions.explanation: Metes = measures (the bearings and distances of consecutive lines); bounds = adjoiners (recital calls to record/physical lines or neighboring parcels). The other options confuse these with monuments, area, or jurisdiction.

quiz_questions.question_text: A deed's metes-and-bounds description is so vague that a surveyor cannot locate the parcel on the ground. What is the most serious legal consequence?
options: The conveyance may be void for lack of locatability
The grantor automatically keeps the minerals
The acreage controls over all other calls
The county must re-survey the parcel for free
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The conveyance may be void for lack of locatability
quiz_questions.explanation: Every conveyance of real property requires a sufficient description; if the lands are not sufficiently described the conveyance can be void for lack of locatability. The other options are not legal consequences of an insufficient description.

quiz_questions.question_text: True or False: A boundary line created only by words in a legal document is less binding than one a surveyor physically runs on the ground.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.correct_answer: False
quiz_questions.explanation: A boundary line may be created by words in a legal document, and that line is as binding and as legal as if it had been run on the ground by a surveyor. This is exactly why descriptions must be unambiguous.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which document should a surveyor NOT rely on as the primary source of a property description?
options: A tax statement or tax deed
A warranty deed
A recorded subdivision plat
An original GLO field note
quiz_questions.correct_answer: A tax statement or tax deed
quiz_questions.explanation: Tax statements and tax deeds are not complete deeds; they must not be used as the basis for a survey or legal document or as the primary source of a property description. The other three are proper source documents.

quiz_questions.question_text: A description reads “Commencing at the northwest corner of said survey; thence S 00°10' W, 330.00 feet to the point of beginning; thence …” Which statement is correct?
options: The northwest corner is the point of commencement and is not a corner of the parcel; the parcel begins 330 ft away at the POB
The northwest corner is the point of beginning and a corner of the parcel
There are two points of beginning in this description
The 330.00-foot course is a perimeter boundary of the parcel
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The northwest corner is the point of commencement and is not a corner of the parcel; the parcel begins 330 ft away at the POB
quiz_questions.explanation: “Commencing at” marks the POC (a reliably found point, here a survey corner), and the 330-ft tie course leads to the POB — the first true corner of the parcel. The tie course is not part of the perimeter, and a description has only one POB.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why does a Texas caption recite an original survey name and an abstract number instead of Section/Township/Range?
options: Texas land was never federal public domain, so it has no PLSS grid; titles are keyed to original GLO land-grant surveys and abstract numbers
Texas law forbids using the metric system
Abstract numbers are easier to remember than section numbers
Section/Township/Range is reserved for water boundaries in Texas
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Texas land was never federal public domain, so it has no PLSS grid; titles are keyed to original GLO land-grant surveys and abstract numbers
quiz_questions.explanation: Texas retained its public lands at annexation and was never federal public domain, so it has no township–range PLSS grid. Land is organized by original land-grant surveys administered by the GLO, identified by abstract number, which the caption recites.

quiz_questions.question_text: Under 22 TAC §138.91, the courses in a description must be referenced to what?
options: An identifiable and monumented line or an established geodetic system
The surveyor's truck odometer
The nearest interstate highway
Magnetic north only
quiz_questions.correct_answer: An identifiable and monumented line or an established geodetic system
quiz_questions.explanation: 22 TAC §138.91(c) requires courses to be referenced to an identifiable and monumented line or an established geodetic system (today commonly the Texas State Plane Coordinate System, NAD83). Magnetic north alone is the historic, less reliable basis the rule moved away from.

quiz_questions.question_text: When a limiting caption (“All that part of the NW1/4 of the NW1/4 … described as follows”) conflicts with a body that runs beyond that quarter-quarter, what is the likely result?
options: The limiting caption may cap the conveyance, so the land beyond the quarter-quarter may not pass
The body always controls, so all the extra land passes regardless
The deed is automatically void
The acreage statement decides the conflict
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The limiting caption may cap the conveyance, so the land beyond the quarter-quarter may not pass
quiz_questions.explanation: Although the body generally controls a conflicting caption, a caption that sounds limiting (“all that part of …”) can restrict what is conveyed; land beyond the recited quarter-quarter may not pass even if the body describes it.

quiz_questions.question_text: Convert the bearing S 35° 00' W to an azimuth measured clockwise from north.
quiz_questions.explanation: A southwest bearing converts as Az = 180° + β = 180° + 35° = 215°.

quiz_questions.question_text: A course has a forward azimuth of 70°. What is its back azimuth?
quiz_questions.explanation: Because the forward azimuth is less than 180°, add 180°: 70° + 180° = 250°.

quiz_questions.question_text: The reverse (back) bearing of a line whose forward bearing is N 22° 30' E is:
quiz_questions.explanation: A back bearing keeps the same angle but flips both letters: the reverse of N 22°30' E is S 22°30' W (the same line viewed from the opposite end).

quiz_questions.question_text: For a course bearing N 60° 00' E with a distance of 200.00 ft, which expressions give its latitude and departure?
options: Lat = 200·cos 60° (north), Dep = 200·sin 60° (east)
Lat = 200·sin 60° (north), Dep = 200·cos 60° (east)
Lat = 200·tan 60°, Dep = 200·cot 60°
Lat = 200·cos 60° (east), Dep = 200·sin 60° (north)
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Lat = 200·cos 60° (north), Dep = 200·sin 60° (east)
quiz_questions.explanation: Latitude (N–S component) = D·cosβ and departure (E–W component) = D·sinβ, where β is the bearing angle. The N and E signs come from the NE quadrant.

quiz_questions.question_text: Compute the departure (east–west component, in feet) of a course bearing N 53° 00' E for a distance of 150.00 ft. Report magnitude to two decimals.
quiz_questions.explanation: Departure = D·sinβ = 150.00 × sin(53°) = 150.00 × 0.79864 ≈ 119.80 ft (eastward, since the bearing is to the East).

quiz_questions.question_text: True or False: In a quadrant bearing such as N 42°10' E, the angle 42°10' is measured from due East.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.correct_answer: False
quiz_questions.explanation: A quadrant bearing is measured from the north or south meridian, not from east or west. N 42°10' E is 42°10' measured east of due north.

quiz_questions.question_text: A traverse has ΣLat = +0.30 ft and ΣDep = −0.40 ft. What is the linear misclosure (ft, two decimals)?
quiz_questions.explanation: Linear misclosure = √(ΣLat² + ΣDep²) = √(0.30² + 0.40²) = √(0.09 + 0.16) = √0.25 = 0.50 ft.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which three requirements must a metes-and-bounds description satisfy to be legally sufficient?
options: It must close, be locatable on the ground, and be unambiguous
It must be notarized, recorded, and indexed
It must state the price, the date, and the grantor
It must use varas, list minerals, and avoid curves
quiz_questions.correct_answer: It must close, be locatable on the ground, and be unambiguous
quiz_questions.explanation: Sufficiency requires that the description close (no gap/overlap), be locatable by a competent surveyor, and unambiguously describe one parcel — all reinforced by 22 TAC §138.91. The other lists describe recording mechanics or unrelated content.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which call is the WEAKEST (lowest dignity) when calls in one description conflict?
options: Quantity / area
Natural monument
Artificial monument
Course (bearing)
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Quantity / area
quiz_questions.explanation: The dignity of calls ranks natural monument > artificial monument > adjoinder > course > distance > quantity/area. Stated acreage is the least reliable — a check, not a control.

quiz_questions.question_text: A called-for, identifiable, undisturbed iron pipe is found 4.1 ft beyond the record distance of 300.00 ft along the same bearing. Applying the dignity of calls, what controls the corner location?
options: The found monument (the corner is held at the pipe, ~304.1 ft)
The record distance (the corner is held at exactly 300.00 ft)
The stated area of the parcel
The midpoint between the pipe and the record distance
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The found monument (the corner is held at the pipe, ~304.1 ft)
quiz_questions.explanation: An artificial monument that is called for, identifiable, and undisturbed controls over a conflicting distance. The corner is held at the found pipe; the record 300.00 ft becomes controlling only if the monument is lost.

quiz_questions.question_text: True or False: In Texas, the current surveying standards of practice are found in 22 TAC §663.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.correct_answer: False
quiz_questions.explanation: Chapter 663 was repealed. After HB 1523 (2019) created TBPELS, the standards were re-codified into 22 TAC Chapter 138 (Subch. E, §§138.81 et seq.), effective 2021. Citing §663 in 2026 is outdated.

quiz_questions.question_text: In State v. Franks (Tex. Civ. App. 1937), which surveyor's work did the court credit in fixing the senior survey's north line?
options: The surveyor who followed the footsteps of the original surveyor and began from recognized original corners
The surveyor with the newest GPS equipment
The surveyor who used the largest claimed acreage
The junior survey, because it was more clearly written
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The surveyor who followed the footsteps of the original surveyor and began from recognized original corners
quiz_questions.explanation: The court adopted Atkinson's retracement because he began from recognized corners and followed the footsteps of the original surveyor; it rejected surveys that did not. A junior survey must conform to the senior's lines and calls.

quiz_questions.question_text: A historic Texas field note calls “N 10° E, 500 varas.” Convert the distance to feet (two decimals), using the statutory vara.
quiz_questions.explanation: Using the statutory vara of 33⅓ in = 2.7778 ft: 500 × 2.7778 = 1388.90 ft.

quiz_questions.question_text: Who is custodian of Texas's original land-grant records (patents, field notes, sketches) that a surveyor must consult when retracing an original survey?
options: The General Land Office (GLO)
The federal Bureau of Land Management (BLM)
The county tax assessor
The Texas Department of Transportation
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The General Land Office (GLO)
quiz_questions.explanation: The Texas GLO holds the original land-grant archive. The BLM keeps federal PLSS records (irrelevant in Texas, which retained its public lands); the tax assessor and TxDOT are not grant-record custodians.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which clause is the correct device to withhold from a conveyance a parcel previously sold, thereby preserving a senior right?
options: EXCEPTING THEREFROM
TOGETHER WITH
SUBJECT TO
RESERVING THEREFROM
quiz_questions.correct_answer: EXCEPTING THEREFROM
quiz_questions.explanation: “EXCEPTING THEREFROM” omits a part of the thing granted and is an important device for preserving senior rights. “Reserving” keeps a new right/easement; “subject to” recognizes an existing right; “together with” adds a right.

quiz_questions.question_text: True or False: Because the Spanish vara measured about 32.9 inches, a Texas surveyor should use 32.9 inches when converting Texas grant varas.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.correct_answer: False
quiz_questions.explanation: Texas fixed the vara by statute at exactly 33⅓ inches (Art. 5730, 1919). Applying a foreign vara value (Spanish ~32.9 in, Mexican ~32.99 in) to a Texas grant yields wrong distances and acreage.

quiz_questions.question_text: Under Texas law, who may prepare and seal a metes-and-bounds description and monument the boundary?
options: A Registered Professional Land Surveyor (RPLS) or Licensed State Land Surveyor (LSLS)
Any licensed real estate broker
The county clerk
A professional engineer with no surveying license
quiz_questions.correct_answer: A Registered Professional Land Surveyor (RPLS) or Licensed State Land Surveyor (LSLS)
quiz_questions.explanation: Under Occupations Code Ch. 1071, only an RPLS (or LSLS) may prepare/seal a description and delineate/monument boundaries. State-land originals specifically require the LSLS credential.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why does Texas treat a stated acreage (area) as the least reliable element of a description?
options: It is the lowest call in the dignity hierarchy — a check on the work, not a control over monuments, courses, or distances
Because acreage cannot be computed accurately
Because Texas measures area only in varas
Because the GLO ignores acreage entirely
quiz_questions.correct_answer: It is the lowest call in the dignity hierarchy — a check on the work, not a control over monuments, courses, or distances
quiz_questions.explanation: In the dignity of calls, quantity/area ranks below monuments, course, and distance. It serves as a check, not a control, which is why area is followed by “more or less.”

problem_templates.name: Bearing to azimuth and back-azimuth
problem_templates.question_template: A deed course is recited as {{quad}} {{ang}} degrees {{min}} minutes (a bearing of {{ang}}°{{min}}' measured from the {{ns}} meridian toward the {{ew}}). Express this course as an AZIMUTH measured clockwise from north, in decimal degrees.

solution_steps_template.title: Apply the quadrant rule
solution_steps_template.calculation_template: NE: Az=β; SE: Az=180−β; SW: Az=180+β; NW: Az=360−β (here β={{base:f2}}°)

problem_templates.explanation_template: Convert the quadrant bearing {{quad}} ({{base:f2}}°) to azimuth using the rule for its quadrant. The azimuth is {{_answer}}°. (Its back-azimuth would be this ± 180°.)

images_needed.caption: Compass rose with the four quadrants and the conversion rule Az = β / 180−β / 180+β / 360−β labeled by quadrant.

problem_templates.name: Latitude or departure of a single course
problem_templates.question_template: A course bears {{nsword}} {{ang}} degrees {{ewword}} for a distance of {{dist:f2}} feet. Compute the magnitude of its {{which}} (the {{axis}} component), in feet.

solution_steps_template.title: Pick the right trig function
solution_steps_template.calculation_template: Latitude = D·cosβ (N–S); Departure = D·sinβ (E–W). Here we need the {{which}}.

solution_steps_template.title: Substitute
solution_steps_template.calculation_template: {{dist:f2}} × ({{which}} uses cos for latitude, sin for departure) of {{ang}}°

problem_templates.explanation_template: Resolve the course into components: latitude = D·cosβ and departure = D·sinβ, with β = {{ang}}° and D = {{dist:f2}} ft. The requested {{which}} has magnitude {{_answer}} ft. Its sign would be {{nsword}}/{{ewword}} depending on the quadrant.

images_needed.caption: A single course as the hypotenuse of a right triangle with latitude (D·cosβ) and departure (D·sinβ) legs labeled.

problem_templates.name: Linear misclosure of a traverse
problem_templates.question_template: After summing the latitudes and departures of all courses in a closed traverse, a surveyor finds a closure in latitude of ΣLat = {{sumLat:f2}} feet and a closure in departure of ΣDep = {{sumDep:f2}} feet. Compute the linear misclosure (the straight-line gap back to the point of beginning), in feet.
problem_templates.explanation_template: The linear misclosure is the hypotenuse of the latitude and departure closures: √({{sumLat:f2}}² + {{sumDep:f2}}²) = {{_answer}} ft. In a perfect figure both sums (and the misclosure) would be zero.

images_needed.caption: A traverse that fails to close, with ΣLat and ΣDep drawn as the legs and the misclosure as the hypotenuse gap to the POB.

problem_templates.name: Area of a triangular parcel in acres
problem_templates.question_template: A triangular parcel has a base of {{base:f2}} feet along a road and a perpendicular height (depth) of {{height:f2}} feet. Compute its area in ACRES (1 acre = 43,560 square feet).
problem_templates.explanation_template: A triangle's area is (base × height) / 2 = ({{base:f2}} × {{height:f2}})/2 = {{sqft:f2}} sq ft. Dividing by 43,560 sq ft/acre gives {{_answer}} acres (which the description would state “more or less”).

images_needed.caption: A triangular parcel with its base along a road and the perpendicular height dimensioned, labeled with the area = (b×h)/2 formula.

homework.title: Homework 1 — Write and Check a Texas Metes-and-Bounds Description
homework.prompt:
**Scenario.** You have surveyed a rectangular 4.00-acre tract carved from the parent tract in the **John Doe Survey, Abstract No. 123, Travis County, Texas**. Your basis of bearing is the Texas State Plane Coordinate System (Central Zone, NAD83). The found southwest corner of the original survey is your point of commencement. From that corner you run **N 00° 00' E, 250.00 feet** to the southwest corner of your tract (the point of beginning). The tract is a rectangle **417.42 feet (east–west)** by **417.42 feet (north–south)**; its south and north lines bear due East/West and its east and west lines bear due North/South. A 1-inch iron rod is set at each of the four tract corners.

**Tasks.**
1. **Write the complete description.** Include: a proper Texas caption (survey + abstract + county); a “Commencing at …” tie to the POB; the four bounding courses run clockwise (each as a bearing + distance + “to a 1-inch iron rod set”), each ending in a semicolon; a closing call to the point of beginning; the area in acres “more or less”; the basis of bearing; and an “END OF DESCRIPTION.” Follow the composition standards (capitalize and spell out bearings; do not capitalize general directions; colon after the caption, period at the end).
2. **Verify closure.** Compute the latitude and departure of each of the four courses and show that ΣLat = 0 and ΣDep = 0 (a perfect rectangle). Then compute the linear misclosure and confirm it is 0.00 ft.
3. **Verify the area.** Show that 417.42 × 417.42 sq ft ÷ 43,560 ≈ 4.00 acres, justifying the “more or less.”
4. **Convert to varas.** Re-state the 417.42-foot south line in varas (to two decimals) using the statutory Texas vara, as an original 1850s field note might have written it.
5. **Compliance check.** Identify which subsections of 22 TAC Ch. 138 your description satisfies (unambiguous identification; found vs. set monuments; reference to a monumented line/geodetic system) and state who (RPLS or LSLS) is required to seal it, and why.

images_needed.caption: Plan of the 4.00-acre square tract inside the John Doe Survey (A-123), showing the found original survey corner as POC, the 250.00-ft tie to the POB, the four iron rods at the tract corners, and the clockwise course directions.

homework.title: Homework 2 — Critique a Defective Description (written + applied)
homework.prompt:
Below is a (deliberately flawed) draft description. Read it critically, then complete the analysis.

> *“That part of Section 12 in the county, beginning at the old oak at the corner of the Smith place; thence about 60 rods to a cedar tree; thence S 30° W to a stake; thence East 400 feet, more or less, to the iron pipe; thence back to the beginning. Containing 5 acres per the tax statement.”*

**Part A — Sufficiency audit (written).** For each of the three sufficiency requirements (must close, must be locatable, must be unambiguous), state whether this description passes or fails and exactly why. Quote the offending language.

**Part B — Defect list.** List at least **five** specific defects, drawing on the composition standards and principles from the module (examples to look for: no caption tie to a GLO survey/abstract, vague/gone monument as the POB, “about” distances, missing basis of bearing, reliance on a tax statement for area, a directional run that may not close, a corner potentially placed on a curve, etc.). For each defect, name the rule it violates.

**Part C — Dignity-of-calls scenario.** Suppose on the ground you find the called-for iron pipe 6.0 ft beyond the recited “400 feet.” State which call controls and why, citing the dignity-of-calls hierarchy and the role of the original surveyor's footsteps (reference *State v. Franks* or *Stafford v. King*).

**Part D — Rewrite (applied).** Rewrite the description correctly for a Texas parcel: invent reasonable values, give it a proper Texas caption (survey + abstract + county), a “Commencing at” tie to a found corner, four clean courses with monument calls run clockwise, a closing call, an area “more or less,” a stated basis of bearing, a call for the survey, and certification language. Keep it to one parcel and make sure it closes.

images_needed.caption: A sketch of the flawed parcel showing the gone “old oak” POB, the vague “about 60 rods” course, and the iron pipe found 6 ft past the recited distance, annotated to support the defect audit.
